original_abstract: This paper examines the evolution and implementation of bilingual (Czech-Polish) nomenclature on railway lines in the Zaolzie region of the Czech Republic. Following a brief historical overview of the Polish national minority in the area, the study analyzes the activities of key organizations, such as the Congress of Poles in the Czech Republic, in advocating for linguistic rights. I explore the legal and theoretical framework governing bilingualism—specifically the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages—and the statutory conditions required for the introduction of minority language signage. The core of the research provides a practical chronological account of the transition to bilingualism at specific railway stations, documenting the shift from initial proposals in 2004 to the current status quo. Furthermore, the paper investigates the broader use of Polish in the railway environment, including digital timetables and audio station announcements recorded by professional lectors. Finally, I address persistent challenges, such as administrative hurdles, inconsistent signage formats, and the socio-political factors that continue to impact the progress of bilingualism in the region today.
humanized_abstract: Abstract: The paper deals with the development and implementation of the Czech-Polish bilingual nomenclature on railway lines in Zaolzie (the Czech part of historic Cieszyn Region) in the Czech Republic. After a short overview of history of the Polish national minority in Zaolzie, we present the activities of the Congress of Poles Abroad (CMP) dealing with issues of linguistic rights of minorities. We describe the legal and theoretical basis of bilingualism (such as the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages) and the statutory regulations that need to be fulfilled before minority language can be introduced into road signs. The central part of the paper represents a detailed, strictly chronological presentation of the progress of bilingual nomenclature on railway stations along the main railway line connecting cities Čadca and Dolny Kubín in the Czech Republic. It shows development of proposals since 2004 up to the present state of affairs. In addition, we also observe the usage of the Polish language in the environment of the railway station, e.g. at electronic departure and arrival boards in stations or in audio station announcements read by professional lecturers. Finally, we address the administrative obstacles arising in connection with the introduction of bilingualism, as well as the incomprehension of non-uniformity of the naming in the form of different types of signs and, last but not least, some political aspects.
